message.content:
Choosing between Python and Rust for a high-performance web backend is a classic trade-off between **development velocity** and **execution efficiency**. Neither language is objectively "better"; the right choice depends on your team's expertise, project timeline, and specific performance requirements.

Here’s a detailed comparison weighing the tradeoffs:

### 1. Performance & Resource Efficiency

| Feature | Python | Rust |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Execution Speed** | Slow. Interpreted language with a Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) in CPython, limiting true multi-threading. | Extremely fast. Compiles to native machine code. No runtime overhead. |
| **Memory Usage** | High. Objects have significant overhead; garbage collection is non-deterministic. | Low. Manual memory management via ownership/borrowing; no garbage collector. |
| **Concurrency** | Limited by GIL. Async I/O (asyncio) is good for I/O-bound tasks but not CPU-bound. | Excellent. Fearless concurrency with threads, async/await, and no data races at compile time. |
| **Scalability** | Scales vertically (more powerful servers) or via many processes (Gunicorn/uWSGI). | Scales horizontally and vertically with minimal resource footprint per request. |

**Verdict:** Rust wins decisively on raw performance, memory efficiency, and handling high-concurrency workloads. Python requires more infrastructure (more servers, more RAM) to handle the same load.

### 2. Development Speed & Productivity

| Feature | Python | Rust |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Learning Curve** | Low. Easy to read and write. Huge talent pool. | Steep. Ownership, borrowing, lifetimes, and the borrow checker require a mental shift. |
| **Development Velocity
